perturbation.original_text: Shareholder hereby covenants and agrees that from the Effective Date until the third (3 rd ) anniversary of the Effective Date (“Restricted Period”), Shareholder will not without the prior written consent of Heritage, engage or participate or have any interest, directly or indirectly, in any Business anywhere in the counties of Santa Clara, Alameda, Contra Costa, Marin, San Francisco and San Mateo located in the State of California (“Restricted Territory”)
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder will endeavor not to engage or participate or have any direct interest in any Business anywhere in the counties of Santa Clara, Alameda, Contra Costa, Marin, San Francisco and San Mateo located in the State of California (“Restricted Territory”)
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'covenants and agrees that ... will not' with 'will endeavor not to' weakens the obligation, making it a goal rather than a strict requirement. Also, adding 'direct' before 'interest' introduces ambiguity, as indirect interests are not clearly defined, potentially undermining the non-compete clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While there are exceptions, weakening the language to 'endeavor not to' makes the enforceability of the agreement more questionable, as it suggests a lower standard of compliance than might be required to meet the exceptions to the law. The statute states that every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'covenants and agrees that ... will not' to 'will endeavor not to' significantly weakens the non-compete obligation. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a) states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The original text imposes a clear restraint, while the changed text, using 'endeavor,' suggests an attempt rather than a strict prohibition. This weakening makes the agreement more vulnerable under Section 16600(a), as it no longer provides a clear restraint, potentially failing to meet the narrow exceptions to the law as stated in 16600(b)(1) which reinforces the voiding of noncompete agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Therefore, Shareholder hereby agrees not to, at any time, disclose to any unauthorized Persons or use for his or its own account or for the benefit of any third party any Confidential Information, whether or not such information is 2 embodied in writing or other physical form or is retained in the memory of Shareholder, without Heritage’s written consent, unless and to the extent that the Confidential Information is or becomes generally known to and available for use by the public other than as a result of Shareholder’s fault or the fault of any other Person bound by a duty of confidentiality to Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, Shareholder agrees to use reasonable efforts not to disclose to unauthorized Persons or use for his or its own account or for the benefit of any third party any Confidential Information, whether or not such information is embodied in writing or other physical form or is retained in the memory of Shareholder, without Heritage’s written consent, unless the Confidential Information is, to Shareholder's knowledge, generally known to the public.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'hereby agrees not to' to 'agrees to use reasonable efforts not to' significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Also, limiting the exception to 'to Shareholder's knowledge' creates a loophole, as the Shareholder could claim ignorance of the information's public availability, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, and the modified language weakens the protection of those secrets by only requiring 'reasonable efforts' to prevent disclosure. This contradicts the purpose of CUTSA, which aims to provide strong legal protection for trade secrets. Also, CUTSA doesn't require the trade secret holder to prove the disclosing party knew the information was generally known to the public. Placing a burden on the trade secret holder to prove such knowledge weakens protections afforded by CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes: Code Search skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> CIV CIV Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search CIV Civil Code - CIV Up^ Back To TOC
perturbation.justification: The change from 'hereby agrees not to' to 'agrees to use reasonable efforts not to' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The addition of 'to Shareholder's knowledge' also creates a loophole. The Law Explanation states this contradicts CUTSA, which provides strong legal protection for trade secrets and doesn't require the trade secret holder to prove the disclosing party knew the information was generally known. The provided Civil Code snippet does not provide any information to the contrary.

perturbation.original_text: Shareholder will, during the Restrictive Period, within ten days after accepting any employment, consulting engagement, engagement as an independent contractor, partnership or other association that might reasonably involve the Business, advise Heritage of the identity of the new employer, client, partner or other Person with whom Shareholder has become associated.
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder should, during the Restrictive Period, within a reasonable time after accepting any employment, consulting engagement, engagement as an independent contractor, partnership or other association, advise Heritage of the identity of the new employer, client, partner or other Person with whom Shareholder has become associated, only if such association is directly related to the Business.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will' to 'should' makes the notification requirement less mandatory. Replacing 'ten days' with 'a reasonable time' introduces vagueness about the deadline. Adding 'only if such association is directly related to the Business' further limits the obligation and creates ambiguity, as what constitutes a 'direct' relationship is not defined.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section of the contract does not directly restrain someone from engaging in a lawful profession, the modification creates ambiguity that makes the contract’s enforceability questionable under California law. The law states that every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. By weakening the requirement to notify Heritage about new employment, and limiting it only to cases 'directly related to the business' the clause can be interpreted to restrain trade more than is permissible under established exceptions to the statute.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated specific actions within a defined timeframe to protect Heritage's business interests. The changed text weakens this obligation by replacing 'will' with 'should,' 'ten days' with 'a reasonable time,' and adding a condition that the association must be 'directly related to the Business.' According to BPC 16600(a), 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' unless an exception applies. By making the notification requirement less mandatory and more ambiguous, the modified contract could be interpreted as an attempt to restrain trade more than permissible, especially given that 'direct' relationship is undefined, increasing the likelihood that it would be deemed unenforceable or used to unduly restrict the Shareholder's future employment options. This alteration creates a condition that may hinder the Shareholder's ability to engage freely in their profession, potentially triggering the restrictions outlined in BPC 16600(a).

perturbation.original_text: Shareholder acknowledges and agrees that the restrictions set forth in this Section 3 are reasonable in scope and essential to the preservation of Diablo’s business and proprietary properties and that enforcement of these restrictions will not cause Shareholder any hardship, and because of Shareholder’s background and experience, will not in any manner preclude Shareholder from becoming gainfully employed in such a manner and to such an extent as will provide a standard of living for Shareholder and the members of Shareholder’s family of at least the sort and fashion to which they have become accustomed.
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder acknowledges that the restrictions set forth in this Section 3 may be considered reasonable and may be essential to the preservation of Diablo’s business. Shareholder hopes that enforcement of these restrictions will not cause Shareholder undue hardship, and believes it is possible that Shareholder may become gainfully employed in such a manner and to such an extent as will provide a reasonable standard of living.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'are reasonable' to 'may be considered reasonable,' 'are essential' to 'may be essential', 'will not cause ... any hardship' to 'hopes that enforcement ... will not cause undue hardship', and 'will not in any manner preclude ... standard of living ... to which they have become accustomed' to 'it is possible that Shareholder may become gainfully employed ... reasonable standard of living' weakens Shareholder's agreement that the terms are acceptable and do not place an undue burden. This makes it easier for the Shareholder to later argue the restrictions are unreasonable or cause hardship.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. For a non-compete to be enforceable, the terms must be reasonable. By weakening the language regarding reasonableness and hardship, the court may find that the non-compete is void. The modified section suggests a reluctance of the shareholder to accept the agreement without issues.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification:
The modified text weakens the shareholder's agreement regarding the reasonableness and lack of hardship of the non-compete clause, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The original text uses definitive language ('are reasonable,' 'are essential,' 'will not cause...any hardship'), while the changed text uses softer, more uncertain language ('may be considered reasonable,' 'may be essential,' 'hopes that enforcement...will not cause undue hardship').

Snippet 1 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By weakening the shareholder's initial acceptance of the non-compete terms as reasonable and not causing hardship, the modified text makes it more likely a court would find the agreement unenforceable under BPC 16600 because it suggests the shareholder does not fully agree that the restrictions are reasonable, and could later argue undue hardship.
